title: Spread syntax should be preferred instead of apply()
description: Spread syntax should be used instead of "apply()"Analyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode SmellQuick FixIDE quick fixes available with SonarQube for IDEWhy is this an issue?More InfoThe spread operator is a more concise and more readable way to pass arguments to a function that takes a variable number of arguments (variadic function). Prior to ES2015, the only way to call such functions with a variable number of arguments was to use the.apply()method.foo.apply(undefined, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply() foo.apply(null, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply() obj.foo.apply(obj, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply()Using.apply()is no longer necessary in such cases - replace it with a spread operato
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

compliant:
foo.apply(undefined, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply()
foo.apply(null, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply()
obj.foo.apply(obj, args); // Noncompliant: use spread syntax instead of .apply()

checks.message: Use spread syntax (...args) instead of .apply() for better readability and performance
